titleEn: Let the Days Do What They Will
translationEn: Let the days do what they will, and be content in soul when fate decrees. Do not despair at the events of the nights, for the events of this world do not endure. Be a man resolute in the face of terrors, and let your character be generosity and faithfulness. If your faults among people are many, and you wish them to have a covering, then veil them with generosity, for every fault is covered, as they say, by generosity. Never show humiliation to your enemies, for the gloating of enemies is an affliction. Do not hope for generosity from a miser, for there is no water in fire for the thirsty. Your sustenance will not be diminished by patience, nor is provision increased by toil.
context: The most iconic poem attributed to Imam al-Shafi'i and one of the most memorized poems in the entire Arabic-speaking world. Its themes of acceptance of fate (tawakkul), noble character, and trust in God's provision resonate deeply with Islamic values. The opening line "Let the days do what they will" is known by virtually every Arabic speaker and is frequently quoted in sermons, speeches, and daily conversation.

titleAr: إِذَا المَرْءُ لَا يَرْعَاكَ إِلَّا تَكَلُّفًا
titleEn: If a Person Only Tends to You Out of Obligation

verses.sadr: إِذَا المَرْءُ لَا يَرْعَاكَ إِلَّا تَكَلُّفًا
فَفِي النَّاسِ أَبْدَالٌ وَفِي التَّرْكِ رَاحَةٌ
verses.ajuz: وَفِي القَلْبِ صَبْرٌ لِلحَبِيبِ وَلَوْ جَفَا

verses.sadr: فَمَا كُلُّ مَنْ تَهْوَاهُ يَهْوَاكَ قَلْبُهُ
verses.ajuz: وَلَا كُلُّ مَنْ صَافَيْتَهُ لَكَ قَدْ صَفَا

verses.sadr: إِذَا لَمْ يَكُنْ صَفْوُ الوِدَادِ طَبِيعَةً
verses.ajuz: فَلَا خَيْرَ فِي وِدٍّ يَجِيءُ تَكَلُّفَا

verses.sadr: وَلَا خَيْرَ فِي خِلٍّ يَخُونُ خَلِيلَهُ

verses.ajuz: وَيُظْهِرُ سِرًّا كَانَ بِالأَمْسِ قَدْ خَفَا

translationEn: If a person only tends to you out of obligation, then leave him and do not grieve excessively over him. Among people there are replacements, and in letting go there is comfort, and in the heart there is patience for the beloved even if he is harsh. Not everyone you love will love you back, nor will everyone you are sincere with be sincere to you. If the purity of affection is not natural, then there is no good in a friendship that comes forced. There is no good in a friend who betrays his companion and meets him after affection with coldness. He denies a bond whose covenant is old, and reveals a secret that yesterday was hidden.
context: One of Imam al-Shafi'i's most beloved poems about the nature of true friendship. It is widely shared on social media and quoted when people experience betrayal or false friendships. The poem's wisdom about not forcing relationships and recognizing when to let go has made it timeless practical advice across Arab culture.

titleEn: We Blame Our Times Though the Fault Is Ours
translationEn: We blame our times, yet the fault is in us — our times have no fault other than us. We satirize this era without cause, yet if time could speak it would satirize us. The wolf does not eat the flesh of another wolf, yet we devour one another openly. We have donned the skins of sheep for deception, yet we are the wolves and people are the victims. He reproaches me for abandoning sins, but no one truly abandons sins completely.
context: A searing self-critique that holds a mirror to society. The opening line "We blame our times though the fault is ours" became one of the most quoted Arabic proverbs and is used to this day whenever people complain about their circumstances. The wolf metaphor — that wolves do not eat each other but humans do — is particularly powerful and widely cited.

titleEn: I Have Tested the People of This World

verses.sadr: بَلَوْتُ بَنِي الدُّنْيَا فَلَمْ أَرَ فِيهِمُ
verses.ajuz: سِوَى مَنْ غَدَا وَالبُخْلُ مِلْءُ إِهَابِهِ

verses.sadr: فَلَا ذَا يَرَانِي وَاقِفًا عِنْدَ بَابِهِ
verses.ajuz: وَلَا ذَا يَرَانِي قَاعِدًا عِنْدَ دَارِهِ

verses.sadr: غَنِيٌّ بِلَا مَالٍ عَنِ النَّاسِ كُلِّهِمْ
verses.ajuz: وَلَيْسَ الغِنَى إِلَّا عَنِ الشَّيْءِ لَا بِهِ

translationEn: I have tested the people of this world and found among them none but those who go about with stinginess filling their skins. So I drew from the sheath of contentment a sharp sword and cut off my hope from them with its blade. So neither does this one see me standing at his door, nor that one see me sitting at his house. I am rich without wealth, independent of all people — for true wealth is independence from things, not possession of them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: blade edge, tip of a sword

context: One of Imam al-Shafi'i's most celebrated poems on self-reliance and dignity. The metaphor of drawing the "sword of contentment" to cut off dependence on people is breathtaking in its imagery. The final verse defines true wealth as independence from need — a concept central to Islamic spirituality (al-ghina 'an al-nas). This poem is frequently quoted in Friday sermons.

titleEn: Lions Die of Hunger in the Forests
translationEn: Lions die of hunger in the forests, while the meat of sheep is eaten by dogs. A slave may sleep on silk, while one of noble lineage has dust for his bedding. An ignorant man lives without a care, while a man of knowledge lives without clothes. And of all wonders is that this is the decree of the Lord of Majesty from the Book.
context: Perhaps al-Shafi'i's most thought-provoking poem, exploring the paradoxes of divine provision. The contrast between lions starving while dogs feast, and nobles sleeping on dust while slaves sleep on silk, leads to a profound conclusion: this is all God's decree. Rather than complaining, the poem teaches acceptance of qadr (divine destiny). The lion-and-dog parable is quoted constantly in Arabic discourse about worldly injustice and divine wisdom.

titleEn: Do You Mock Supplication?
translationEn: Do you mock supplication and belittle it, while you know not what supplication has wrought? The arrows of the night never miss their mark, but they have an appointed time, and that time shall be fulfilled.
context: This poem affirms the power of supplication (du'a) and warns against dismissing it. Al-Shafi'i likens nighttime prayers to arrows that never miss, emphasizing that divine answers come at their appointed time.

titleEn: People Have Said Much About Women

verses.ajuz: قرب من لا تحب جهد البلاء

translationEn: People have spoken at length about women and said that the love of women is the hardest trial. But loving women is not the hardship; rather, being near one you do not love is the true affliction.
context: This poem offers a witty rebuttal to those who disparage love, arguing that the real misery is being forced into the company of someone you do not love. It reflects al-Shafi'i's sharp insight into human relationships.

titleEn: What Grief for the Youth

verses.sadr: عمر الفتى لو كان في كفه

translationEn: What grief for the youth who lives even an hour after losing his dear ones! If a man's life were in his own hand, he would cast it away after losing those he loved.
context: This short elegy expresses the profound grief of outliving one's beloved companions. Al-Shafi'i captures the universal anguish of bereavement with striking brevity.

titleEn: I Have Become Cast Aside Among the Ignorant

verses.ajuz: في العقل فرق وفي الآداب والحسب

verses.sadr: والعود لو لم تطب منه روائحه
verses.ajuz: لم يفرق الناس بين العود والحطب

translationEn: I have become cast aside among people who knew not the right of the learned, so they sold the head for the tail. People are gathered together, yet between them lies a difference in intellect, manners, and lineage. Just as pure gold shares its color with brass, yet gold is superior. And had the oud wood not given off its fragrance, people would not distinguish between oud and firewood.
context: This poem laments the failure of people to recognize true merit, using vivid metaphors of gold versus brass and oud versus firewood. Al-Shafi'i asserts that inner worth, not outward appearance, is what distinguishes people.

titleEn: The Fire of My Soul Dimmed as My Temples Grayed

verses.sadr: أيا بومة قد عششت فوق هامتي
verses.ajuz: على الرغم مني حين طار غرابها

verses.sadr: أأنعم عيشا بعد ما حل عارضي
ولا تمشين في منكب الأرض فاخرا

verses.ajuz: كما لاح في ظهر الفلاة سرابها

translationEn: The fire of my soul dimmed as my temples blazed with gray, and my night darkened when its meteor shone bright. O owl that nested atop my head against my will, when its raven flew away. You saw my ruins and visited me. Pay the alms-tax of prestige and know that it is due. Shall I enjoy life after gray has settled on my cheeks, when pleasant days have turned bitter? Leave aside the evil deeds, for they are forbidden for the pious soul to commit. In a short while the earth will contain you, just as the alms-tax becomes due when the threshold is met. Be good to the noble and you will own their loyalty, for the best trade is earning goodwill. Do not walk upon the earth boastfully, for its sweetness and torment are driven toward us. I have seen this world as nothing but delusion and falsehood, like a mirage gleaming in the open desert. It is but a rotting carcass around which dogs vie to snatch. If you avoid it, you will be at peace with its people; if you grab at it, its dogs will fight you for it. Blessed is the soul that devoted itself to the depths of its home, with doors shut and curtains drawn.
context: This lengthy poem reflects on aging and the deceptive nature of worldly life. Al-Shafi'i uses powerful imagery of gray hair, mirages, and carcasses to urge withdrawal from worldly pursuits and devotion to spiritual seclusion.

titleEn: When a Vile Person Insults Me, I Rise Higher

verses.ajuz: وما العيب إلا أن أكون ماببه

verses.sadr: ولو لم تكن نفسي علي عزيزة

verses.ajuz: وعار على الشبعان إن جاع صاحبه

translationEn: When a vile person insults me, I only rise higher, for the real disgrace would be to stoop to his level. Had my soul not been precious to me, I would have let it fight every wretch. And if I strove only for my own benefit, you would find me most negligent in what I seek. But I strive to benefit my companion, for it is a shame for the well-fed if his companion goes hungry.
context: This poem illustrates al-Shafi'i's philosophy of dignified restraint and generosity. He teaches that true nobility lies in rising above insults and caring for others before oneself.

titleEn: The Fool Addresses Me With Every Foulness
translationEn: The fool addresses me with every foulness, yet I refuse to respond to him. The more foolish he becomes, the more forbearing I grow, like oud wood that only grows more fragrant when burned.
context: One of al-Shafi'i's most celebrated couplets, this poem uses the brilliant metaphor of oud incense that becomes more fragrant when burned. It encapsulates the Islamic virtue of hilm (forbearance) in the face of provocation.

titleEn: Among the Afflictions Is to Love
translationEn: Among the afflictions is that you love, yet the one you love does not love you back. He turns his face away from you, while you persist and never leave him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to leave him, to be absent from him

context: This poem in the truncated kamil meter speaks to the pain of unrequited love. Al-Shafi'i captures the helplessness of loving someone who does not reciprocate, a theme that resonated across Arabic literary tradition.

titleEn: Tell the Astrologer About Me

verses.sadr: عالما أن ما يكون وما كان

translationEn: Tell the astrologer on my behalf that I disbelieve in what the stars have decreed. I know that what comes to pass and what has been is a decree from the Almighty that must be fulfilled.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Almighty, the Guardian (name of God)

context: This poem firmly rejects astrology and affirms Islamic monotheism. Al-Shafi'i declares that destiny belongs to God alone, not to the stars, reflecting his scholarly position against superstitious practices.

titleEn: You Are Sufficient for Me

verses.ajuz: ولحسبي إن صح لي فيك حسب

verses.sadr: لا أبالي متى ودادك لي صح

translationEn: You are sufficient for me, and in You the heart finds its sufficiency, and my honor is complete if I have standing with You. I care not, once Your love for me is true, what hardships time may bring.
context: This devotional poem expresses complete reliance upon God (tawakkul). Al-Shafi'i declares that divine love renders all worldly trials insignificant, embodying the spiritual confidence of a true scholar of faith.

titleEn: When You Are Confused Between Two Matters
translationEn: When your affair bewilders you between two matters, and you cannot tell which is wrong and which is right, then oppose your desire, for desire leads souls to what is blameworthy.
context: This widely quoted couplet offers a practical moral guideline: when uncertain, go against your desires. It reflects the Islamic emphasis on disciplining the nafs (ego) and al-Shafi'i's juridical wisdom.

titleAr: أرى الغر في الدنيا إذا كان فاضلا
titleEn: I See the Noble in This World, if Virtuous

verses.sadr: أرى الغر في الدنيا إذا كان فاضلا
وإن كان مثلي لا فضيلة عنده

translationEn: I see the noble person in this world, if he is virtuous, ascend above the heads of men and be sought after. But if he is like me, possessing no distinction, he is compared to a child playing in the streets.
context: In this poem, al-Shafi'i reflects on the relationship between virtue and social standing with characteristic humility. His self-deprecation is a literary device emphasizing that merit, not birth, determines one's worth.

titleAr: ما في المقام لذي عقل وذي أدب
titleEn: There Is No Rest for the Wise and Cultured

verses.sadr: ما في المقام لذي عقل وذي أدب

verses.ajuz: وانصب فإن لذيذ العيش في النصب
إن ساح طاب وإن لم يجر لم يطب

verses.sadr: والأسد لولا فراق الأرض ما افترست
verses.ajuz: والسهم لولا فراق القوس لم يصب

verses.sadr: والشمس لو وقفت في الفلك دائمة
verses.ajuz: لملها الناس من عجم ومن عرب
والعود في أرضه نوع من الحطب

translationEn: There is no rest in staying put for the wise and cultured, so leave your homeland and travel abroad. Travel and you will find replacements for those you leave behind; toil, for the sweetness of life lies in toil. I have seen that stagnant water spoils; if it flows it becomes sweet, and if it does not flow it turns foul. The lion, had it not left its land, would never have hunted prey; the arrow, had it not left the bow, would never have hit its mark. The sun, had it stayed fixed in the sky, would bore all people, Arab and non-Arab alike. Raw gold is like dust lying in its place, and oud wood in its native land is just a type of firewood. But when the gold travels far, it becomes precious, and when the oud travels, it becomes valued like gold.
context: One of al-Shafi'i's most famous poems, it celebrates travel and the pursuit of knowledge abroad. Each couplet offers a natural metaphor -- water, lions, arrows, the sun, gold, oud -- to argue that greatness requires leaving one's comfort zone.

titleEn: I Shall Journey Across the Length and Breadth of Lands
translationEn: I shall journey across the length and breadth of lands, to attain my goal or die a stranger. If my soul perishes, then may God honor it; and if it survives, the return home will be near.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: how excellent it is (expression of praise)

context: This poem captures the spirit of determination and willingness to sacrifice for one's aspirations. Al-Shafi'i himself traveled extensively from Gaza to Mecca, Yemen, Iraq, and Egypt in pursuit of knowledge.

titleEn: He Who Respects Men, They Will Respect Him
translationEn: He who respects men, they will hold him in awe; and he who belittles men will never be respected. He whose rights men have upheld is fortunate; and he who defies men will not succeed.
context: This poem articulates the principle of reciprocity in human relations. Al-Shafi'i teaches that respect begets respect, and contempt for others leads to one's own humiliation.

titleAr: لما عفوت ولم أحقد على أحد
titleEn: When I Forgave and Bore No Grudge

verses.sadr: لما عفوت ولم أحقد على أحد

verses.ajuz: كما إن قد حشى قلبي محبات

translationEn: When I forgave and bore no grudge against anyone, I relieved my soul from the burden of enmities. I greet my enemy when I see him, to ward off evil from myself with salutations. I show cheerfulness to the person I dislike most, as though my heart were filled with love for him. People are a disease, and the disease of people is their proximity; yet in withdrawing from them lies the severing of bonds.
context: This poem reveals al-Shafi'i's pragmatic approach to social relations: forgive to free yourself, be courteous even to enemies, yet recognize that human company itself can be toxic. The final verse reflects a tension between social necessity and spiritual solitude.

titleAr: يا لهف نفسي على مال أفرقه
titleEn: How I Wish I Had Wealth to Distribute

verses.sadr: يا لهف نفسي على مال أفرقه
إن اعتذاري إلى من جاء يسألني
verses.ajuz: ما ليس عندي لمن إحدى المصيبات

translationEn: How I wish I had wealth to distribute among the poor who possess noble character! Indeed, having to apologize to one who comes asking me for what I do not have is one of the greatest calamities.
context: This poem expresses the pain of being unable to help those in need despite wanting to. Al-Shafi'i highlights generosity as a core value and portrays the inability to give as a personal affliction.

titleEn: The Judges of the Age Have Gone Astray
translationEn: The judges of the age have gone astray, and their loss has become manifest. They sold their religion for worldly gain, and their trade did not profit.
context: This sharp critique targets corrupt judges who trade religious integrity for worldly gain. Al-Shafi'i, as a leading jurist, was particularly concerned with the integrity of the judiciary and Islamic legal scholarship.

titleEn: Money Made People Speak After Their Silence
translationEn: Money made people speak after their silence, people who had been quiet before. Yet they showed no kindness to anyone through virtue, nor did they uphold any noble quality.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: made to speak, gave voice to

context: This poem critiques the corrupting influence of wealth on people's character. Al-Shafi'i observes that money gives the unworthy a voice while failing to instill true virtue or generosity in them.

titleEn: The Prophet's Family Are My Means
translationEn: The Prophet's family are my means, and they are my path to him. Through them I hope that tomorrow I shall be given my record in my right hand.
context: This poem expresses al-Shafi'i's love for the Prophet's household (Ahl al-Bayt), which famously brought accusations of Shi'ism against him. He defended himself by saying that if love of the Prophet's family is Rafidism, then let all know he is a Rafidi.

titleAr: اصبر على مر الجفا من معلم
titleEn: Endure the Bitterness of a Teacher's Harshness

verses.sadr: اصبر على مر الجفا من معلم
ومن لم يذق مر التعلم ساعة

verses.ajuz: إذا لم يكونا لا اعتبار لذاته

translationEn: Endure the bitterness of a teacher's harshness, for the retention of knowledge lies in his strictness. He who does not taste the bitterness of learning for an hour will swallow the humiliation of ignorance for his entire life. He who misses education in his youth -- say the funeral prayer over him four times for his death. A man's essence, by God, is in knowledge and piety; without these two, there is no regard for his person.
context: This celebrated poem on the value of education is among al-Shafi'i's most quoted. The metaphor of performing funeral prayers over an uneducated person powerfully conveys that a life without learning is a kind of death.

titleEn: I Love Among Brothers Every Agreeable One

verses.sadr: فمن لي بهذا ؟ ليت أني أصبته

translationEn: I love among brothers every agreeable one, and every one who turns a blind eye to my faults. One who agrees with me in everything I wish, and guards my honor in life and after death. But who can provide me such a friend? If only I found him, I would share with him my store of good deeds. I examined my brothers and found that the fewest of them, despite their great number, were truly trustworthy.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who lowers his gaze (overlooking faults)

context: This poem explores the ideal of true friendship and the difficulty of finding it. Al-Shafi'i's conclusion that few among many friends are truly trustworthy reflects his hard-won life experience of scholarly rivalries and political persecution.

titleEn: What Will Your Guest Tell His Family?
translationEn: What will your house-guest tell his family, if asked about his return and his coming? Will he say: I crossed the Euphrates and did not quench my thirst at his door, though its waves overflowed? I climbed the steps of glory, but its mountain passes and valleys narrowed against what I desired. My neediness will be revealed by my flattery, just as a glass reveals the impurities in its water. I possess rubies and pearls of poetry, and I wear the crown and diadem of speech. Its blossoms surpass the garden of the hills, and its brocade shimmers in the assembly of generosity. The eloquent poet is a black serpent, and poetry is its venom and spittle. The enmity of poets is an intractable disease, but the noble one finds its cure easy.
context: This poem is a qasida-like piece where al-Shafi'i reflects on hospitality, poetry, and the power of the spoken word. The comparison of the eloquent poet to a venomous serpent highlights the dangerous power of language.

titleEn: Many a Calamity That Overwhelms a Man
translationEn: Many a calamity overwhelms a man beyond his capacity, yet with God lies the way out. It became dire, and when its links were tightly locked, it was relieved -- and I had thought it would never be relieved.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its links, its rings (of a chain)

context: This beloved poem offers hope in times of despair. Al-Shafi'i uses the metaphor of a tightly locked chain to describe how relief comes precisely when hardship reaches its peak, echoing the Quranic promise that with hardship comes ease.

titleEn: Beautiful Patience -- How Near Is Relief!

verses.ajuz: من راقب الله في الأمور نجا

verses.sadr: من صدق الله لم ينله أذى

translationEn: Beautiful patience -- how near is relief! He who is mindful of God in all affairs is saved. He who is truthful with God will not be harmed, and he who places his hope in Him will find what he hoped for.
context: This poem is a concise statement of trust in divine providence (tawakkul). Al-Shafi'i connects beautiful patience (sabr jamil) -- a Quranic concept from the story of Prophet Ya'qub -- with the certainty of divine relief.

titleAr: قالوا سكت وقد خوصمت قلت لهم
titleEn: They Said: You Were Silent When Quarreled With

verses.sadr: قالوا سكت وقد خوصمت قلت لهم
والصمت عن جاهل أو أحمق شرف
أما ترى الأسد تخشى وهي صامتة ؟

translationEn: They said: You were silent when quarreled with. I told them: A response is a key that opens the door to evil. Silence before an ignorant or foolish person is an honor, and in it too lies the preservation of one's dignity. Do you not see that the lion is feared while it is silent, while the dog is despised, by my life, though it barks?
context: This poem employs the powerful contrast between the silent lion and the barking dog to justify dignified silence. Al-Shafi'i argues that responding to fools opens the door to further evil, making silence the wiser and nobler course.

titleEn: Be Both a Jurist and a Sufi, Not Just One

verses.sadr: فذلك قاس ، لم يذق قلبه تقى
verses.ajuz: وهذا جهول ، كيف ذو الجهل يصلح ؟

translationEn: Be both a jurist and a Sufi, not just one of the two, for I sincerely advise you, by God. The one who is only a jurist is hard-hearted, his heart has not tasted piety; and the other is ignorant -- how can one who is ignorant be reformed?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a jurist, expert in Islamic law

context: This poem famously bridges the divide between Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh) and Sufism (tasawwuf). Al-Shafi'i argues that law without spiritual refinement produces harshness, while spirituality without knowledge leads to ignorance.

titleEn: The Trials of Time Are Many and Unending
translationEn: The trials of time are many and unending, while its joys come to you only like holidays -- rare and fleeting. Wealth enslaves the great and subjugates them, yet you see it as a slave in the hands of the lowly.
context: This poem contrasts the abundance of trials with the rarity of joy, comparing happiness to holidays that come only occasionally. The second verse offers a paradox about wealth enslaving the noble while serving the ignoble.

titleEn: They Said: You Have Become a Rafidi
translationEn: They said: You have become a Rafidi (Shi'ite rejecter). I said: Never! Rejection is neither my religion nor my creed. But I have, without doubt, pledged my loyalty to the best of imams and the best of guides. If loving the Wali (Ali) is Rafidism, then let mankind know that I am indeed a Rafidi.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the saint, the ally (referring to Ali)

context: This is one of al-Shafi'i's most famous poems, where he defends his love for Imam Ali and the Prophet's family against accusations of Shi'ism. As a Sunni scholar, his stance demonstrates that love for Ahl al-Bayt transcends sectarian boundaries.

titleEn: If Only Dogs Were Our Neighbors

verses.ajuz: وليتنا لا نرى مما نرى أحدا
تبق سعيدا إذا ما كنت منفردا

translationEn: If only dogs were our neighbors instead of people, and if only we did not see anyone of those we see! For dogs are loyal guides in their territory. You would remain happy if you were alone.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to guide, to show the way

context: This provocative poem expresses deep disillusionment with human company, arguing that the loyalty of dogs surpasses that of most people. It reflects al-Shafi'i's bitter experiences with betrayal and the value he placed on solitude.

titleAr: تمنى رجال أن أموت وإن أمت
titleEn: Men Wished That I Would Die

verses.sadr: تمنى رجال أن أموت وإن أمت
فقل للذي يبغي خلاف الذي مضى

translationEn: Men wished that I would die, but if I die, that is a path in which I am not alone. So say to the one who seeks something other than what has passed: prepare yourself for another like it, for it is as though it has already come.
context: A defiant poem in which al-Shafi'i addresses those who wished for his death, reminding them that death is universal and inevitable. The final verse warns his enemies that the same fate awaits them, displaying the poet's characteristic composure in the face of hostility.

titleEn: When I Came to People Seeking from Them

verses.ajuz: وناديت في الأحياء هل من مساعد ؟

verses.sadr: فلم أر فيما ساءني غير شامت
verses.ajuz: ولم أر فيما سرني غير حاسد

translationEn: When I came to people seeking among them a trustworthy brother in times of hardship, I went through life in ease and difficulty, and called out among the living: is there anyone to help? But in my misfortune I found none but those who gloated, and in my joy I found none but the envious.
context: A poignant reflection on the rarity of true friendship. Al-Shafi'i describes how through both prosperity and hardship, he found only gloaters in misfortune and enviers in joy. This poem captures the loneliness of the scholar and the disillusionment with fair-weather companions.

titleAr: إني صحبت أناسا ما لهم عدد
titleEn: I Have Accompanied Countless People

verses.sadr: إني صحبت أناسا ما لهم عدد
verses.ajuz: وكنت أحسب أني قد ملأت يدي
كالدهر في الغدر لم يبقوا على أحد
وإن مرضت فخير الناس لم يعد

translationEn: I accompanied countless people and thought I had filled my hands with companions. But when I tested my friends, I found them like time in their treachery — loyal to no one. If I am absent, the worst of people insult me, and if I fall ill, the best of people do not visit. If they see me in good fortune, my joy saddens them, and if they see me in misfortune, my distress delights them.
context: One of al-Shafi'i's most biting poems about the nature of false friendships. The systematic enumeration of betrayals — insults in absence, neglect in illness, envy in success, and glee in misfortune — paints a devastating portrait of human fickleness that resonates across centuries.

titleEn: He Who Exhausts His Camels Traveling to a Land

verses.ajuz: لو كان يعلم غيبا مات من كمد

verses.sadr: من كان لم يؤت علما في بقاء غد
verses.ajuz: ماذا تفكره في رزق بعد غد

translationEn: How many exhaust their camels traveling joyfully to a land, while death seeks them from that very land. And how many laugh while death hovers above their heads — if they knew the unseen, they would die of grief. He who has not been given knowledge of whether tomorrow will come, why does he worry about the provision of the day after?
context: A powerful meditation on mortality and the futility of excessive worldly ambition. Al-Shafi'i observes the irony of people striving toward destinations where death awaits them, laughing while fate looms overhead. The final verse delivers a logical argument for trust in God: if you cannot even know whether you will live until tomorrow, why worry about tomorrow's provision?

titleAr: عفا الله عن عبد أعان بدعوة
titleEn: May God Pardon the Servant Who Helped with a Prayer

verses.sadr: عفا الله عن عبد أعان بدعوة
إلى أن مشى واشي الهوى بنميمة
verses.ajuz: إلى ذاك من هذا فزالا عن العهد

translationEn: May God pardon the servant who helps with a prayer two close friends who were constant in their affection, until a slanderer of passions walked with gossip from one to the other, and they broke their covenant.
context: A brief but moving poem about the destructive power of gossip (namimah) on friendships. Al-Shafi'i laments how a slanderer can destroy a lifelong bond between two close friends, reflecting an important Islamic ethical teaching against tale-bearing, which is repeatedly condemned in the Quran and hadith.

titleAr: إن كنت تغدو في الننوب جليدا
titleEn: If You Go About Boldly in Sins

verses.sadr: إن كنت تغدو في الننوب جليدا
لا تيأسن من لطف ربك في الحشا
لو شاء أن تصلى جهنم خالدا

translationEn: If you go about boldly in sins yet fear the threat of the Day of Return, then indeed the pardon of the Sovereign has come to you, and He has showered upon you ever more blessings. Do not despair of your Lord's kindness — He created you in your mother's womb as a mere embryo. Had He willed that you burn eternally in Hell, He would not have inspired your heart with monotheism.
context: A deeply consoling poem that embodies the Islamic balance between fear of God and hope in His mercy. The final verse contains a powerful theological argument: the very fact that God guided your heart to faith (tawhid) is proof that He did not intend to abandon you. This poem is frequently quoted to those suffering from spiritual despair.

titleEn: If I Have My Day's Provision When Morning Comes

verses.ajuz: فأترك ما أريد لما ما أريد

translationEn: If I have my day's provision when morning comes, then let worry depart from me, O Sa'd. Let not the worries of tomorrow cross my mind, for tomorrow has its own new provision. I submit when God wills a matter, and I leave what I desire for what He desires.
context: A succinct expression of tawakkul (trust in God's provision) that captures the essence of Islamic contentment. The poem teaches that if one has enough for today, there is no need to worry about tomorrow, for God provides anew each day. The final verse embodies complete surrender to the divine will.

titleEn: Were It Not That Poetry Disgraces Scholars
translationEn: Were it not that poetry disgraces scholars, I would today be a greater poet than Labid. And braver in battle than every lion, and the family of Muhallab and the sons of Yazid. And were it not for the fear of the Most Merciful, my Lord, I would consider all people my servants.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Labid ibn Rabi'ah, legendary pre-Islamic poet

context: A rare poem of self-pride (fakhr) from al-Shafi'i, where he boasts of his poetic talent, courage, and stature while tempering it all with piety. The claim that only God-consciousness prevents him from considering all people his servants is both audacious and deeply humble, as it shows that his restraint comes from faith, not weakness.

titleEn: I See Rest for the Truth When It Is Fulfilled

verses.ajuz: ويثقل يوما إن تركت على عمد

verses.sadr: وحسبك حظا أن ترى غير كاذب
verses.ajuz: وقولك لم أعلم وذاك من الجهد

verses.sadr: ومن يقض حق الجار بعد ابن عمه

verses.ajuz: وإن نابه حق أتوه على قصد

translationEn: I see rest for the truth when it is fulfilled, but it grows heavy when deliberately neglected. Your best fortune is to be seen as truthful, and to say "I did not know" — that is from diligence. Whoever fulfills the rights of his neighbor after his cousin, and his closest companion in nearness and distance, will live as a master whose memory people find sweet, and if a right befalls him, they come to him willingly.
context: A poem on fulfilling obligations and maintaining truthfulness. Al-Shafi'i argues that discharging one's duties brings relief, while neglecting them compounds the burden. The hierarchy of rights — to relatives, neighbors, and companions — reflects Islamic social ethics, and the promise that a righteous person becomes beloved by all echoes Prophetic teachings.

titleEn: Man Wishes to Be Given His Desires
translationEn: Man wishes to be given his desires, but God refuses all except what He has willed. Man says "my profit and my wealth," yet the fear of God is the best thing one can gain.
context: A concise two-verse poem that contrasts human desire with divine will. The first verse establishes that God's plan overrides human wishes, while the second redefines true profit as taqwa (God-consciousness) rather than material wealth. This poem is widely quoted in Islamic sermons as a reminder of spiritual priorities.

titleAr: يا من يعانق دنيا لا بقاء لها
titleEn: O You Who Embrace a World That Does Not Last

verses.sadr: يا من يعانق دنيا لا بقاء لها
إن كنت تبغي جنان الخلد تسكنها
verses.ajuz: فينبغي لك أن لا تأمن النارا

translationEn: O you who embrace a world that does not last, spending your evenings and mornings as a traveler in it. Why did you not leave the embrace of this world so that you may embrace maidens in Paradise? If you seek the gardens of eternity to dwell in, then you should never feel safe from the Fire.
context: A poem of ascetic admonishment (wa'z) that calls upon the reader to abandon attachment to the transient world in favor of the eternal hereafter. The rhetorical question in the second verse and the warning in the third reflect the Islamic principle of balancing hope in Paradise with fear of Hellfire.

titleEn: Rain Down Pearls, O Mountains of Sarandib

verses.sadr: أنا إن عشت لست أعدم قوتا

translationEn: Rain down pearls, O mountains of Sarandib, and overflow with gold, O wells of Takrur. If I live, I will never lack sustenance, and if I die, I will never lack a grave. My ambition is the ambition of kings, and my soul is the soul of a free man who sees humiliation as disbelief. And if I am content with my provision for life, then why should I visit Zayd or Amr?
context: One of al-Shafi'i's most spirited declarations of independence and self-respect. He addresses the fabled treasure lands of his time — Sarandib (Sri Lanka) for gems and Takrur (West Africa) for gold — saying he needs none of it. The declaration that humiliation is equivalent to disbelief (kufr) reflects his fierce dignity, and the rhetorical "why should I visit Zayd or Amr?" means he will not beg from anyone.

titleAr: الدهر يومان ذا أمن وذا خطر
titleEn: Time Is Two Days: One of Safety, One of Peril

verses.sadr: الدهر يومان ذا أمن وذا خطر
verses.ajuz: والعيش عيشان ذا صفو وذا كدر

verses.sadr: أما ترى البحر تعلو فوقه جيف
وفي السماء نجوم لا عداد لها

translationEn: Time is two days: one of safety and one of peril, and life is two lives: one of clarity and one of turbidity. Do you not see how corpses float upon the sea while pearls settle at the depths of its floor? And in the sky there are stars beyond counting, yet only the sun and the moon are eclipsed.
context: A brilliant poem employing natural metaphors to explain why the great are tested while the insignificant are left alone. The image of corpses floating on the surface while pearls rest at the bottom, and the observation that only the sun and moon are eclipsed among countless stars, are among the most widely quoted Arabic sayings about the nature of envy and trials.

titleEn: I Found My Silence to Be a Profitable Trade

verses.ajuz: إذا لم أجد ربحا فلست بخاسر

verses.sadr: وما الصمت إلا في الرجال متاجر

translationEn: I found my silence to be a profitable trade, so I adhered to it — if I find no profit, at least I am not at a loss. Silence among men is nothing but a trade, and its merchant rises above every other merchant.
context: A celebrated couplet on the virtue of silence, employing the metaphor of trade. Al-Shafi'i presents silence as a zero-risk investment: at worst you break even, at best you profit immensely. This echoes the Prophetic hadith "Whoever believes in God and the Last Day, let him speak good or remain silent."

titleEn: The Lame One Grew Proud and Danger Rose with Him

verses.ajuz: فقل له خير ما استعملته الحذر
ولم تخف سوء ما تأتي به القدر

translationEn: The lame one grew proud and danger rose with him — tell him that the best course is caution. You thought well of the days when they were good to you, and you did not fear the evil that fate may bring. The nights were peaceful with you, so you were deceived by them, yet when nights are clearest, turbidity appears.
context: A warning poem addressed to someone who has become arrogant in good times. Al-Shafi'i cautions that prosperity can be deceptive and that calamity often strikes at the height of comfort. The final verse — "when nights are clearest, turbidity appears" — became a widely quoted Arabic proverb about the unpredictability of fortune.

titleEn: Accept the Excuses of Those Who Come Apologizing

verses.ajuz: إن ير عندك فيما قال : أو فجرا

translationEn: Accept the excuses of those who come to you apologizing, whether what they say seems righteous or sinful. The one who pleases you openly has obeyed you, and the one who disobeys you in secret has still honored you.
context: A pearl of interpersonal wisdom advising generosity in accepting apologies, regardless of their sincerity. Al-Shafi'i argues that the mere act of someone coming to apologize — even with a dubious excuse — is itself a form of respect. The one who hides his disobedience still shows deference by not openly defying you.

titleAr: إذا ما كنت ذا فضل وعلم
titleEn: If You Are a Person of Virtue and Knowledge

verses.sadr: إذا ما كنت ذا فضل وعلم

translationEn: If you are a person of virtue and knowledge in what the ancients and moderns have disagreed upon, then debate with the one you debate in calmness, with forbearance — neither insisting nor being arrogant. Let him benefit you with what he has learned, without condescension, from subtle points and rare insights. Beware of the obstinate one and the one who shows off saying "I have won," and the one who boasts. For the evil in such behavior leads to severance of ties and mutual aversion.
context: A poem on the ethics of scholarly debate (munazarah), a topic close to al-Shafi'i's heart as the founder of systematic legal methodology. He advocates for calm, humble discourse and warns against debating for victory rather than truth. This poem is frequently cited in Islamic academic contexts as a model for scholarly conduct.

titleAr: إذا لم أجد خلا تقيا فوحدتي
titleEn: If I Cannot Find a Pious Friend, Then My Solitude

verses.sadr: إذا لم أجد خلا تقيا فوحدتي

translationEn: If I cannot find a pious friend, then my solitude is sweeter and more pleasant than keeping company with a deviant. And I sit alone in worship, secure — more settled in my life than with a companion I must be wary of.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I am wary of, cautious of

context: A poem that praises beneficial solitude over harmful companionship. Al-Shafi'i's preference for being alone with God over being with untrustworthy company reflects both his personal experience of betrayal and the Islamic emphasis on choosing righteous friends. The poem is often quoted to comfort those who find themselves without trustworthy companions.

titleAr: كن سائرا في ذا الزمان بسيره
titleEn: Walk in This Age with Its Own Gait

verses.sadr: كن سائرا في ذا الزمان بسيره
verses.ajuz: وعن الورى كن راهبا في ديره

verses.sadr: إني اطلعت فلم أجد لي صاحبا
verses.ajuz: أصحبه في الدهر ولا في غيره

translationEn: Walk in this age with its own gait, and among people be like a monk in his monastery. Wash your hands of this era and its people, and beware of their affection so you may attain its good. I looked around and found no companion to accompany in this life or any other. So I left the lowest of them for the abundance of their evil, and I left the highest of them for the scarcity of their good.
context: A poem advocating social withdrawal as a survival strategy. Al-Shafi'i uses the striking image of a monk in his cell to describe how one should relate to society. The devastating conclusion — abandoning both the lowest people for their evil and the highest for their insufficient good — reveals a profound disillusionment with human society.

titleEn: A Friend Who Does Not Help on a Day of Hardship
translationEn: A friend who does not help on a day of hardship is, by any measure, close to an enemy. Friends do not last in every age, nor do brothers, except for mutual consolation. I have spent my life earnestly seeking a trustworthy brother, but the search itself has exhausted me. The lands and their people have become unrecognizable, as if their inhabitants are no longer human.
context: A poem on the impossibility of finding true friendship. Al-Shafi'i declares that a friend who fails you in need is essentially an enemy, and that the endless search for a trustworthy companion can itself become an exhausting distraction. The final verse's lament that people have become inhuman captures a deep social alienation.

titleEn: My Heart, O God, Is Illuminated by Your Mercy

verses.sadr: وما تقلبت من نومي وفي سنتي

verses.ajuz: ولم تكن فاضحي فيها بفعل مسي
تجعل علي إذا في الدين من لبس
ويوم حشري بما أنزلت في عبس

translationEn: My heart, O God, is illuminated by Your mercy in secret and in public, at dawn and in the darkness before dawn. I do not turn in my sleep or slumber except that Your remembrance is between each breath. You have bestowed upon my heart the knowledge that You are God, the Lord of blessings and holiness. I have committed sins that You know of, yet You did not expose me for my misdeeds. So bestow upon me the remembrance of the righteous, and do not let there be confusion in my faith. And be with me throughout my worldly life and my afterlife, and on the day of my gathering, by what You revealed in Surah Abasa.
context: One of al-Shafi'i's most intimate devotional poems, a direct supplication to God. The imagery of God's remembrance accompanying every breath and every turn in sleep is deeply moving. The confession of sins coupled with gratitude for God's concealment of them reflects the Islamic concept of God as al-Sattar (the Concealer of faults).

titleAr: يا وعظ الناس عما أنت قاعله
titleEn: O You Who Preach to People Against What You Yourself Do

verses.sadr: يا وعظ الناس عما أنت قاعله
verses.ajuz: يا من يعد عليه العمر بالنفس
إن السفينة لا تجري على اليبس
ما كنت تركب من بغل ومن فرس

verses.sadr: يوم القيامة لا مال ولا ولد

translationEn: O you who preach to people against what you yourself do, O you whose life is counted breath by breath. Guard your youth from stains that defile it, for white cloth bears little stain. Like one who washes the clothes of others while his own garment drowns in filth and impurity. You seek salvation yet do not walk its path — a ship does not sail on dry land. Being carried on the funeral bier will make you forget riding the mules and horses you used to ride. On the Day of Judgment there is no wealth and no children, and the constriction of the grave makes one forget the wedding night.
context: A searing critique of religious hypocrisy that remains as relevant today as when it was composed. Al-Shafi'i addresses the preacher who admonishes others while neglecting his own soul, using the vivid image of a laundryman with filthy clothes. The poem builds to a powerful climax reminding the reader of death's inevitability and the Day of Judgment.

titleEn: Extracting a Tooth and Being Imprisoned
translationEn: Extracting a tooth, and being imprisoned, and the departure of the soul, and bringing back yesterday; and the biting cold, and leading alone, and tanning a hide without sun; and eating a lizard, and hunting a fly, and grinding grain in a barren land; and blowing fire, and bearing disgrace, and selling a house for a fraction of a penny; and selling a shoe, and losing a companion, and beating a friend with a thick rope — all of these are easier than a free man standing at the door of a wretched person, hoping for a gift.
context: A tour de force of rhetorical accumulation in which al-Shafi'i lists increasingly absurd and painful experiences, only to declare them all easier than a dignified person begging at the door of a miserable man. The rapid-fire listing technique and the devastating punchline make this one of his most memorable poems on self-dignity and the refusal to beg.

titleEn: Knowledge Is the Nursery of All Pride

verses.ajuz: من همه في مطعم أو ملبس

verses.sadr: إلا أخو العلم الذي يعنى به

translationEn: Knowledge is the nursery of all pride — beware lest you miss the pride of that nursery. Know that knowledge is not attained by one whose concern is food or clothing. Only the devotee of knowledge who dedicates himself to it in both conditions — naked or clothed — attains it. So make for yourself an abundant share of it, and forsake for its sake the sweetness of sleep and rest. Perhaps one day, when you attend a gathering, you will be its leader and its pride.
context: A motivational poem on the pursuit of knowledge that is still quoted in Arabic academic circles today. Al-Shafi'i argues that true knowledge requires sacrificing physical comforts — food, clothing, and sleep — and that only those who pursue it in all conditions will attain it. The promise that such dedication leads to leadership in scholarly assemblies reflects his own remarkable life trajectory.

titleAr: شهدت بأن الله لا رب غيره
titleEn: I Bear Witness That God Has No Lord Besides Him

verses.sadr: شهدت بأن الله لا رب غيره

verses.ajuz: وكان أبو حفص على الخير يحرص

translationEn: I bear witness that God has no lord besides Him, and I testify that the Resurrection is true, and I am sincere. And that the bonds of faith are clear speech and righteous deeds that may increase and decrease. And that Abu Bakr is God's successor, and Abu Hafs (Umar) was eager for goodness. And I testify before my Lord that Uthman is virtuous, and that Ali possesses a specialized excellence. They are leaders by whose guidance people are guided — may God curse whoever belittles them.
context: A creedal poem in which al-Shafi'i affirms core Sunni beliefs: monotheism, the Resurrection, and the legitimacy of all four Rightly Guided Caliphs in order. The statement that faith "increases and decreases" reflects the Shafi'i theological position. This poem was important historically in defending al-Shafi'i against accusations of Shi'i sympathies.

titleEn: I Complained to Waki' About My Poor Memory
translationEn: I complained to Waki' about my poor memory, so he guided me to abandon sins. And he informed me that knowledge is light, and the light of God is not granted to a sinner.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Waki' ibn al-Jarrah, famous hadith scholar

context: Perhaps the single most quoted poem attributed to al-Shafi'i, recounting his conversation with his teacher Waki' ibn al-Jarrah. The connection drawn between sin and impaired memory, and between knowledge and divine light, has profoundly shaped Islamic educational philosophy. Students across the Muslim world memorize this couplet as a reminder that moral purity is a prerequisite for scholarship.

titleAr: إذا لم تجودوا والأمور بكم تمضى
titleEn: If You Are Not Generous While Affairs Go Your Way

verses.sadr: إذا لم تجودوا والأمور بكم تمضى

translationEn: If you are not generous while affairs go your way and your hands hold the power to give and withhold, then what can be expected of you when you are removed from power and the world bites you with its fangs? And the days reclaim what they gave you, for it is the habit of the days to reclaim the loan.
context: A poem addressed to those in positions of power, urging generosity while they still can. Al-Shafi'i warns that worldly fortune is merely a loan that the days will reclaim, and that stinginess in prosperity reveals a character that will be even worse in adversity. The metaphor of the world "biting with its fangs" is striking and memorable.

titleEn: Advise Me in Private

verses.ajuz: فلا تجزع إذا لم تعط طاعه

translationEn: Advise me in private when we are alone, and spare me advice in public. For advice given among people is a form of rebuke that I am not willing to hear. And if you go against me and disobey my words, then do not be upset if you are not given obedience.
context: One of al-Shafi'i's most quoted poems on the etiquette of giving advice (nasihah). The principle that public advice is tantamount to public humiliation became a cornerstone of Islamic social ethics. This poem is frequently cited in discussions about how to enjoin good and forbid evil without causing harm or embarrassment.

titleEn: If a Man Is Wise and Pious
translationEn: If a man is wise and pious, his piety will occupy him from noticing the faults of others. Just as the ailing sick person is occupied by his own pain from noticing the pain of all other people.
context: A compact poem on self-focus and avoiding preoccupation with others' faults. Al-Shafi'i uses the powerful analogy of a sick person who is too absorbed in his own pain to notice others' suffering. This reflects the Islamic teaching that a truly pious person is too busy working on his own shortcomings to criticize others.

titleEn: Sufficient for Me Is the Knowledge That It Benefits
translationEn: Sufficient for me is the knowledge that it benefits: humiliation lies only in greed. Whoever is mindful of God turns back from the evil he used to do. No bird ever flew and rose high except that, just as it flew up, it fell.
context: A poem of remarkable brevity and density, each verse containing a complete proverb. The declaration that humiliation lies only in greed encapsulates al-Shafi'i's philosophy of self-sufficiency. The final verse about the bird that must eventually descend is a metaphor for the impermanence of worldly elevation and has become a widely quoted Arabic proverb.

titleEn: How Many an Oppressor I Was Spared From Fighting
translationEn: How many an oppressor I was spared from fighting, for destiny brought him down with a mighty fall. My only weapons in Islam were worship and supplications that cannot be blocked by shields. It is enough that the oppressor escapes while behind him fly arrows of prayer from the bows of those bowing in worship — arrows feathered with the lashes of every wakeful worshipper, their tips drenched in tears.
context: A powerful poem on the spiritual weapon of supplication (du'a) against oppression. Al-Shafi'i uses vivid military imagery — arrows, bows, shields — to describe how the prayers of the oppressed, offered in the stillness of night with tears, are more effective than any physical weapon.

titleEn: You Disobey God While Claiming to Love Him
translationEn: You disobey God while outwardly claiming to love Him — this is logically impossible and truly astounding. If your love were sincere, you would obey Him, for the lover is obedient to the one he loves. Every day He bestows upon you a new blessing from Him, yet you neglect to give thanks for it.
context: One of al-Shafi'i's most widely shared poems on social media and in Islamic circles. It exposes the contradiction between claiming to love God while disobeying His commands. The second verse — "the lover is obedient to the one he loves" — has become a famous standalone aphorism in Islamic ethics.

titleAr: سل المفتي المكي من آل هاشم
titleEn: Ask the Meccan Mufti from the House of Hashim

verses.sadr: سل المفتي المكي من آل هاشم
verses.ajuz: إذا اشتد وجد بامرىء كيف يصنع
فليس له شيء سوى الموت أنفع

translationEn: Ask the Meccan mufti from the House of Hashim: when yearning overwhelms a person, what should he do? He treats his passion, conceals his anguish, and endures with patience in all affairs, submitting. Each day he swallows a bitter draught, and nothing is more relieving for him than death.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bitter draught, lump in the throat

context: A rare ghazal (love poem) attributed to al-Shafi'i, where he speaks of concealed love and its anguish. The poem is notable for its framing — asking a Meccan religious authority about love — which blends the sacred and romantic in a way characteristic of early Islamic poetry.

titleEn: The Slave Is Free If He Is Content
translationEn: The slave is free if he is content, and the free man is a slave if he is greedy. So be content and do not covet, for nothing disgraces a person like greed.
context: A concise masterpiece of two verses that has become one of the most quoted Arabic aphorisms. The paradox of the free slave and the enslaved free man encapsulates al-Shafi'i's philosophy that true freedom lies in contentment (qana'a), not in social status. It is widely used in Islamic sermons on the dangers of greed.

titleEn: He Adorned the Lands and All Upon Them
translationEn: He adorned the lands and all upon them — the Imam of the Muslims, Abu Hanifa — with rulings, traditions, and jurisprudence like the verses of the Psalms upon a page. Neither in the East is there his equal, nor in the West, nor even in Kufa itself. May the mercy of our Lord be upon him forever, as long as days pass and a page is read.
context: A remarkable poem of praise from al-Shafi'i for Imam Abu Hanifa, the founder of the Hanafi school of law. This poem demonstrates the deep mutual respect among the great imams of Islamic jurisprudence, despite their differences in legal methodology. It is frequently cited to promote unity among the four Sunni schools of thought.

titleEn: The Eagle Eats Carrion by Its Strength
translationEn: The eagle, despite its strength, eats the carrion of the wilderness, while the fly, though weak, gathers honey.
context: A single-verse poem that carries the weight of an entire philosophy. Al-Shafi'i uses the contrast between the powerful eagle eating carrion and the weak fly gathering honey to illustrate that provision (rizq) is not determined by strength or status but by God's decree. It echoes the theme of his famous lion-and-dog poem.

titleAr: ارحل بنفسك من أرض تضام بها
titleEn: Depart from a Land Where You Are Oppressed

verses.sadr: ارحل بنفسك من أرض تضام بها
verses.ajuz: ولا تكن من فراق الأهل في حرق
في أرضه وهو مرمي على الطرق

translationEn: Depart from a land where you are oppressed, and do not burn with grief over leaving your people. Raw ambergris is dung in its homeland, but when it travels abroad it is carried upon necks. Kohl is a type of stone — you see it in its land thrown upon the roads. But when it traveled abroad, it gained all excellence, and came to be carried between the eyelid and the pupil.
context: One of al-Shafi'i's most famous poems on the virtue of travel and emigration. The twin metaphors of ambergris and kohl — both worthless in their homeland but precious abroad — are among the most celebrated images in Arabic poetry. The poem encourages those who feel undervalued to seek new environments where their worth will be recognized.

titleEn: My Wakefulness to Refine Knowledge Is Sweeter to Me

verses.ajuz: في الدرس أشهى من مدامة ساق

translationEn: My wakefulness to refine knowledge is sweeter to me than the embrace of a beautiful woman and the pleasure of a hug. The scratching of my pens upon their pages is more delightful than the singing of lovers. More pleasurable than a maiden striking her tambourine is my tapping to shake the sand from my papers. My swaying with joy at solving a difficult problem in study is more intoxicating than the wine of a cupbearer. I spend the night awake through the darkness while you sleep through it — and then you seek to keep pace with me?
context: One of the most celebrated Arabic poems on the love of knowledge and scholarship. Al-Shafi'i compares the joy of studying to every worldly pleasure — romance, music, wine — and finds it superior. The final verse is a powerful challenge to those who sleep while scholars labor through the night. This poem is regularly quoted in academic and educational contexts across the Arab world.

titleEn: If You Hear That a Fortunate Man Acquired

verses.sadr: لكن من رزق الحجا حرم الغني
إن الذي رزق اليسار فلم ينل
verses.ajuz: أجرا ولا حمدا لغير مو فق

translationEn: If you hear that a fortunate man acquired a dry stick and it bore fruit in his hands, believe it. And if you hear that a deprived man came to water to drink but it dried up, accept it as true. If wealth came through clever schemes, you would find me clinging to the stars at the edges of the sky. But he who is given intelligence is denied wealth — two opposites that are utterly separated. The most deserving of God's creation of worry is an ambitious man afflicted with meager provision. And among the proofs of fate and its wisdom is the misery of the wise and the comfort of the fool. He who is given ease but gains neither reward nor praise is simply not granted success. Fortune brings near every distant matter, and fortune opens every closed door.
context: An extended meditation on the paradox of divine provision (rizq) and human effort. Al-Shafi'i argues that wealth and intelligence are often mutually exclusive, and that fate determines outcomes regardless of schemes. The verse "the misery of the wise and the comfort of the fool" has become a proverbial expression in Arabic culture.

titleEn: If a Man Reveals His Secret with His Own Tongue

verses.sadr: إذا ضاق المرء عن سير نفسه

translationEn: If a man reveals his secret with his own tongue, then blames others for it, he is a fool. If a man's own chest is too narrow to contain his secret, then the chest of the one he confides in is even narrower.
context: A sharp two-verse poem on the responsibility of keeping one's own secrets. Al-Shafi'i points out the absurdity of blaming others for revealing what you yourself could not keep hidden. This wisdom has become proverbial in Arabic culture and is often quoted in discussions about trust and discretion.

titleEn: The Stranger Fears the Thief
translationEn: The stranger endures the fear of a thief, the submission of a debtor, and the humiliation of one in chains. And when he remembers his family and his homeland, his heart flutters like the wings of a frightened bird.
context: A poignant poem on the hardships of living away from home. Despite al-Shafi'i's famous advocacy of travel, this poem reveals the emotional cost of being a stranger. The image of the heart fluttering like a frightened bird's wings when remembering home is among the most tender images in his poetry.

titleAr: توكلت في رزقي على الله خالقي
titleEn: I Have Placed My Trust for Provision in God My Creator

verses.sadr: توكلت في رزقي على الله خالقي
verses.ajuz: وأيقنت أن الله لا شك رازقي

verses.sadr: وما يك من رزقي فليس يفوتني
verses.ajuz: ولو كان في قاع البحار الغوامق
ولو لم يكن مني اللسان بناطق

verses.sadr: ففي أي شيء تذهب النفس خسرة

translationEn: I have placed my trust for provision in God, my Creator, and I am certain that God is undoubtedly my Provider. Whatever is destined as my provision will not escape me, even if it were at the bottom of the deepest seas. God Almighty will bring it by His grace, even if my tongue does not utter a word. So why should the soul grieve over anything, when the Most Merciful has already apportioned the provision of all creatures?
context: A quintessential expression of tawakkul (reliance on God) in Arabic poetry. Al-Shafi'i affirms that provision is guaranteed by God and will reach its intended recipient no matter the obstacles. This poem is among the most memorized and recited of his works, frequently used in sermons and spiritual counseling to ease anxiety about livelihood.

titleAr: لو كنت بالعقل تعطى ما تريد إذن
titleEn: If Intelligence Gave You What You Want

verses.sadr: لو كنت بالعقل تعطى ما تريد إذن
رزقت مالا على جهل فعشت به

translationEn: If intelligence gave you everything you want, then you would have gained nothing from this world by way of provision. You were given wealth despite your ignorance and lived by it — but you are not the first fool to be provided for.
context: A biting two-verse poem that reinforces al-Shafi'i's recurring theme: wealth and intelligence do not necessarily go together. The sardonic closing — "you are not the first fool to be provided for" — delivers a pointed critique of those who mistake their wealth for evidence of their wisdom.

titleAr: علمي معي حيثما يممت فهو معي
titleEn: My Knowledge Is with Me Wherever I Go

verses.sadr: علمي معي حيثما يممت فهو معي
إن كنت في البيت كان العلم فيه معي
verses.ajuz: أو كنت في السوق كان العلم في السوق

translationEn: My knowledge is with me wherever I go — it accompanies me, for my heart is a vessel for it like the inside of a chest. If I am at home, knowledge is there with me; and if I am in the marketplace, knowledge is in the marketplace.
context: A beloved poem celebrating the portability and permanence of knowledge as the true wealth that can never be stolen. Al-Shafi'i contrasts the heart as a container for knowledge with a physical chest, emphasizing that learning is the one possession that accompanies you everywhere. This is frequently quoted in educational settings across the Arab world.

titleAr: رام نفعا فضر من غير قصد
titleEn: He Sought Benefit but Caused Harm Unintentionally

verses.sadr: رام نفعا فضر من غير قصد

translationEn: He sought benefit but caused harm unintentionally — and some acts of kindness turn out to be acts of disobedience.
context: A single-verse gem about unintended consequences. Al-Shafi'i captures a profound truth about human action: that well-intentioned deeds can sometimes lead to harm. This verse is often cited in discussions about the importance of wisdom and foresight alongside good intentions.

titleEn: Nothing Scratches Your Skin Like Your Own Nail
translationEn: Nothing scratches your skin like your own nail, so take charge of all your affairs yourself. And if you seek to fulfill a need, then seek out one who recognizes your worth.
context: A widely quoted poem on self-reliance that has become a proverb in Arabic culture. The first hemistich — "nothing scratches your skin like your own nail" — is used idiomatically to mean no one will solve your problems better than you yourself. The second verse adds practical wisdom: when you must ask for help, choose someone who values you.

titleEn: I Found That Contentment Is the Head of Wealth
translationEn: I found that contentment is the head of wealth, so I held fast to its coattails. Neither does this one see me at his door, nor that one see me preoccupied with him. So I became wealthy without a single dirham, passing among people like a king.
context: A companion piece to al-Shafi'i's famous "I tested the people of this world" poem, expanding on the theme that true wealth lies in self-sufficiency. The final image of walking among people "like a king" without owning a single coin is a powerful expression of spiritual independence. This poem is frequently paired with the hadith "Richness is not having many possessions, but richness is self-contentment."

titleEn: It Is Misfortune That You Love
translationEn: It is misfortune that you love someone while the one you love loves another. Or that you wish good for a person while he wishes you harm.
context: A poignant observation on the pain of unrequited love and misplaced kindness. Al-Shafi'i identifies two of life's greatest miseries: loving someone who loves another, and being kind to someone who wishes you ill. These themes resonate deeply in Arabic culture and the poem is widely shared in contemporary social media.

titleEn: The True Scholar Is the Scholar by His Actions
translationEn: The true scholar is the scholar by his actions, not the scholar by his speech and statements. Likewise, the true leader is the leader by his character, not the leader by his people and men. And likewise, the truly rich is the rich man by his state (of contentment), not the rich man by his possessions and wealth.
context: A poem that redefines three key social roles — the scholar, the leader, and the wealthy — by their inner qualities rather than their outward trappings. Al-Shafi'i insists that true scholarship lies in practice, true leadership in character, and true wealth in contentment. This tripartite structure makes it a favorite in Islamic educational settings.

titleEn: Guard Your Soul and Carry It Upon What Adorns It

verses.ajuz: نبا بك دهر أو جفاك خليل

verses.sadr: و' ن ضاق رزق اليوم فاصبر إلى غد
ولا خير في ود امرئ متلون
verses.ajuz: إذا الريح مالت ، مال حيث تميل

translationEn: Guard your soul and carry it upon what adorns it, and you will live safely with people speaking well of you. Show people only your best face, whether fate turns against you or a friend forsakes you. If today's provision is tight, be patient until tomorrow — perhaps the calamities of time will pass from you. There is no good in the affection of a fickle person — when the wind shifts, he shifts wherever it blows. How many brothers there are when you count them, but in times of adversity they are few.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: showing dignity, putting on a good face

context: A comprehensive guide to dignified living that addresses personal conduct, patience in hardship, and the nature of true friendship. The final verse — "how many brothers when you count them, but in adversity they are few" — has become one of the most frequently quoted Arabic proverbs about the rarity of true friends.

titleEn: Every Time Life Disciplines Me
translationEn: Every time life disciplines me, it shows me the deficiency of my intellect. And whenever I increase in knowledge, it only increases my awareness of my own ignorance.
context: A masterpiece of intellectual humility in just two verses. Al-Shafi'i — one of the greatest minds in Islamic history — confesses that the more he learns, the more he realizes his ignorance. This sentiment echoes Socrates' famous dictum and is widely quoted in Islamic scholarship to encourage humility in the pursuit of knowledge.

titleEn: Seek Knowledge, for No One Is Born a Scholar

verses.ajuz: وليس أخو علم كمن هو جاهل

verses.sadr: وإن كبير القوم لا علم عنده
وإن صغير القوم إن كان عالما

translationEn: Seek knowledge, for no one is born a scholar, and the man of knowledge is not like the ignorant. The elder of a people who has no knowledge is small when the crowds gather around him. And the youth of a people, if he is knowledgeable, is great when assemblies turn to him for counsel.
context: A powerful exhortation to seek knowledge that reverses social hierarchies: the ignorant elder is diminished while the learned youth is elevated. Al-Shafi'i challenges age-based authority by insisting that knowledge, not seniority, is the true measure of a person's worth. This poem is commonly displayed in schools and libraries across the Arab world.

titleEn: Wisdom Cannot Be Attained by One Whose Life
translationEn: Wisdom cannot be attained by one who spends his life toiling for the welfare of his family. Knowledge is only attained by a youth free from worries and preoccupations. Even if Luqman the Wise — whose excellence was carried by caravans far and wide — had been afflicted with poverty and dependents, he would not have distinguished between straw and herbs.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Luqman (the wise man mentioned in the Quran)

context: A provocative argument that serious scholarship requires freedom from worldly burdens. Al-Shafi'i invokes Luqman the Wise — a legendary figure mentioned in the Quran — to argue that even the wisest person would be intellectually diminished by poverty and family obligations. The poem reflects al-Shafi'i's own life of dedicated scholarship and travel in pursuit of knowledge.

titleEn: In Proportion to Effort You Attain Heights

verses.sadr: ومن رام العلا من غير كد

translationEn: In proportion to your effort you attain heights, and whoever seeks glory must stay up through the nights. Whoever seeks glory without effort has wasted his life pursuing the impossible. You seek honor yet sleep through the night — only one who dives into the sea finds pearls.
context: One of the most motivational poems in the Arabic language, frequently recited in schools and quoted in graduation speeches. The opening line — "in proportion to effort you attain heights" — has become a standalone proverb. The pearl-diving metaphor in the final verse powerfully illustrates that achievement requires plunging into difficulty, not merely wishing from the shore.

titleEn: If We Favor Ali, They Call Us Rejectors

verses.sadr: وفضل أبي بكر إذا ما ذكرته
فلا زلت ذا رفض ونصب كلاهما

translationEn: If we favor Ali, they call us Rafidites (rejectors) in the eyes of the so-called virtuous. And if I mention the virtue of Abu Bakr, I am accused of being a Nasibi (anti-Ali partisan) when I speak of merit. So let me remain both a Rafidi and a Nasibi at once — loving them both — until I am laid to rest in the sand.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I am laid to rest, buried

context: A courageous poem on sectarian balance that has become iconic in Sunni theology. Al-Shafi'i declares his love for both Ali (revered by Shia) and Abu Bakr (the first caliph) and refuses to be confined to partisan labels. This poem is one of the most frequently cited texts in inter-sectarian dialogue within Islam.

titleAr: يا آل بيت رسول الله حبكم
titleEn: O Family of the Messenger of God, Your Love

verses.sadr: يا آل بيت رسول الله حبكم
verses.ajuz: فرض من الله في القرآن أنزله
من لم يصل عليكم لا صلاة له

translationEn: O Family of the Messenger of God, your love is an obligation from God, revealed in the Quran. It suffices you as a point of immense pride that whoever does not send blessings upon you, his prayer is not valid.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the family of the household (of the Prophet)

context: Perhaps al-Shafi'i's most famous poem on love for the Prophet's family (Ahl al-Bayt). By declaring this love a Quranic obligation and linking it to the validity of prayer, al-Shafi'i establishes love for the Prophet's family as a core tenet of Sunni Islam. This poem is widely recited in mawlid celebrations and gatherings honoring the Prophet's family.

titleEn: I Treated All People with Tact, but My Envier

verses.ajuz: إذا كان لا يرضيه إلا زوالها

translationEn: I treated all people with tact, but my envier — managing him proved impossible and beyond reach. How can a man deal tactfully with one who envies his blessings, when nothing will satisfy the envier except their disappearance?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I treated with tact, managed diplomatically

context: A sharp psychological insight into the nature of envy (hasad). Al-Shafi'i argues that the envier is uniquely impossible to appease because envy, by definition, desires the destruction of the envied person's blessings. This poem is widely quoted in discussions about dealing with envious people and is considered one of the most precise descriptions of hasad in Arabic literature.

titleEn: I Found That the Companion of Knowledge Is Noble
translationEn: I found that the companion of knowledge is noble, even if his parents were lowly. Knowledge never ceases to elevate him until noble people honor his status. They follow him in every situation, like a shepherd followed by his flock. Were it not for knowledge, no men would have prospered, and neither the lawful nor the forbidden would be known.
context: A powerful argument for knowledge as the great social equalizer. Al-Shafi'i insists that learning ennobles even those of humble origins, and that the learned person naturally becomes a leader whom others follow. The final verse ties knowledge directly to the Islamic legal framework, making scholarship not merely admirable but essential for distinguishing right from wrong.

titleEn: Three Things Are the Destroyers of Mankind
translationEn: Three things are the destroyers of mankind and the callers of the healthy to sickness: constant drinking of wine, constant indulgence in intercourse, and eating food upon food (before the previous meal is digested).
context: A practical health-related poem that reflects al-Shafi'i's holistic concern for human wellbeing beyond purely spiritual matters. The advice about moderation in drink, physical relations, and eating aligns with Islamic principles of balance and echoes Prophetic traditions about health. This poem is sometimes cited in discussions of Islamic medicine and preventive health.

titleEn: Shall I Scatter Pearls Among Grazing Cattle

verses.sadr: لعمري لئن ضيعت في شر بلدة

translationEn: Shall I scatter pearls among grazing cattle, and string gems for shepherds of sheep? By my life, even if I am wasted in a wretched town, I will not waste among them the finest words. If God Almighty in His kindness makes it easy, and I find people worthy of knowledge and wisdom, I will spread knowledge and benefit, and gain their affection in return. Otherwise, my knowledge remains stored with me and stored with them. Whoever gives knowledge to the ignorant wastes it, and whoever withholds it from the deserving has committed injustice.
context: A poem about the ethics of sharing knowledge that sets out a careful balance: knowledge should neither be wasted on the unappreciative nor withheld from the deserving. The final verse has become a famous aphorism in Islamic educational philosophy, establishing a dual obligation on the scholar — to teach the worthy and to refuse to cast pearls before swine.

titleEn: Be Chaste, and Your Women Will Be Chaste

verses.ajuz: كان الزنا من أهل بيتك فاعلم

translationEn: Be chaste, and your women will be chaste in what is sacred, and avoid what is unbefitting of a Muslim. Indeed, adultery is a debt — if you lend it out, know that adultery will come from your own household.
context: This poem delivers a powerful moral lesson on chastity and its reciprocal nature: those who transgress against others' families will find the same befalling their own. Al-Shafi'i frames sin as a debt that inevitably returns to the sinner's household.

titleEn: I Give Generously from What I Have
translationEn: I give generously from what I have, even if I spend the night hungry, my sides folded in and my insides aching. I display the signs of wealth among my companions so my true state remains hidden, though I am destitute. Between myself and God alone I complain of my need in truth, for God knows best the true condition.
context: Al-Shafi'i reveals the dignified concealment of poverty, preferring to give generously even while suffering hunger rather than exposing his need to people. The poem exemplifies the Islamic virtue of tawakkul — relying on God alone while maintaining dignity before others.

titleEn: I Have Tested You and Tried My Own Character
translationEn: I have tested you and tested my own character, and my teacher's instruction of me has been sufficient for you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I tested you / I tried you

context: This single-verse poem is a concise declaration about the relationship between experience, character, and learning. Al-Shafi'i suggests that true knowledge gained through a teacher's instruction serves as both self-refinement and a lesson for others.

titleEn: By the Station of My Humility Before Your Supreme Majesty

verses.ajuz: بمخفي سر لا أحيط به علما

verses.sadr: أذقنا شراب الأنس يا من إذا سقى
verses.ajuz: محبا شرابا لا يضام ولا يظما

translationEn: By the station of my humility before Your supreme majesty, by a hidden secret I cannot encompass in knowledge. By the bowing of my head, by my confession of lowliness, by the extending of my hands, I seek the rain of Your generosity and mercy. By Your beautiful names, whose mere description — for their majesty — exhausts all prose and poetry. By the ancient covenant of "Am I not your Lord?", by the One who was concealed and became known through His names. Let us taste the drink of intimacy, O You who, when You give a lover drink, gives one that knows no humiliation nor thirst.
context: This is a deeply spiritual supplication in which al-Shafi'i pleads before God through successive oaths of humility, invoking the divine names and the primordial covenant (Quran 7:172). The poem represents Shafi'i's mystical side, expressing longing for divine intimacy and spiritual quenching.

titleEn: To You, God of Creation, I Raise My Plea

verses.ajuz: وإن كنت - ياذا المن والجود - مجرما

verses.sadr: فما زلت ذا عفو عن الذنب لم تزل
فيا ليت شعري هل أصير لجنة
فإن تعف عني تعف عن متمرد
يقيم إذا ما الليل مد ظلامه
verses.ajuz: على نفسه من شدة الخوف مأتما

verses.sadr: فصيحا إذا ما كان في ذكر ربه
verses.ajuz: وفي ما سواه في الورى كان أعجما
أخا السهد والنجوى إذا الليل أظلما

verses.sadr: عسى من له الإحسان يغفر زلتي

translationEn: To You, God of creation, I raise my plea, even though I am — O Lord of grace and generosity — a sinner. When my heart hardened and my paths narrowed, I made my hope a ladder to Your forgiveness. My sin seemed enormous to me, but when I measured it against Your pardon, my Lord, Your forgiveness was far greater. You have always been forgiving of sins, never ceasing to bestow grace and pardon as a gift and honor. Were it not for You, no worshipper could withstand Iblis — how could they, when he led astray even Your chosen one, Adam? Oh, would that I knew: shall I end in Paradise, rejoicing, or in the Hellfire, filled with regret? If You pardon me, You pardon a rebellious one, an oppressor and wrongdoer who never leaves sin. And if You punish me, I do not despair, even if my soul were cast into Hell for its crimes. How wondrous is the knowing, noble one whose eyelids overflow with blood from the intensity of his love. When the night spreads its darkness, he holds a vigil of mourning over himself from the severity of fear. Eloquent when remembering his Lord, yet among people in all other matters he is as one struck mute. He remembers days of his youth that have passed and the sins he committed in them through ignorance. He became a companion of sorrow throughout his day, a brother of sleeplessness and whispered prayer when the night darkens. He says: "My Beloved, You are my wish and my desire — You suffice as a goal and a treasure for those who hope." Are You not the One who nourished me and guided me, and You have never ceased bestowing favors and blessings upon me? Perhaps the One to whom all goodness belongs will forgive my slip and conceal my burdens and all that has preceded.
context: This is one of al-Shafi'i's most celebrated poems, a profound supplication that oscillates between fear of divine punishment and hope in God's boundless mercy.
