It paints a vivid portrait of the ideal worshipper — the 'arif (gnostic) — who weeps blood in devotion, stays awake in prayer, and finds eloquence only in the remembrance of God.

titleEn: Knowledge, by Its Grace, for the One Who Serves It
translationEn: Knowledge, by its grace, makes all people servants to the one who serves it. It is therefore obligatory to guard it just as one guards one's honor and blood among people. Whoever possesses knowledge and then, out of ignorance, entrusts it to those unworthy of it has wronged it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its servants / those who serve it

context: Al-Shafi'i elevates knowledge to a sacred trust that ennobles its bearer and must be protected from being shared with the unworthy. This poem reflects the classical Islamic tradition of treating sacred knowledge as an amanah (trust) that requires careful stewardship.

titleEn: I Am Content with My Daily Sustenance
translationEn: I am content with my daily sustenance from my time and have guarded myself from humiliation. Out of fear that people would say so-and-so has favor over so-and-so. Whoever's wealth I have no need of, I do not care if he shuns me. And whoever sees me with an eye of completeness, I see him as complete in virtue.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I am content / I am satisfied
he shunned me / he was cold to me

context: This poem celebrates contentment (qana'a) as a means of preserving dignity and self-respect. Al-Shafi'i declares his independence from others' wealth and establishes a principle of reciprocal respect: those who regard him well are regarded well in return.

titleEn: Guard Your Tongue, O Human Being

verses.sadr: كم في المقابر من قتيل لسانه

translationEn: Guard your tongue, O human being — let it not sting you, for it is a serpent. How many lie in their graves, slain by their own tongue, who were once feared in encounter by their peers.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: it stings you / it bites you

context: Al-Shafi'i uses the vivid metaphor of the tongue as a serpent to warn against careless speech. The poem reminds us that many powerful people met their end not through swords but through the consequences of their own words.

titleAr: ما شئت كان ، وإن لم أشأ
titleEn: What You Will Happens, Even If I Do Not Will It

verses.sadr: ما شئت كان ، وإن لم أشأ
verses.ajuz: وما شئت إن لم تشأ لم يكن

translationEn: What You will comes to be, even if I do not will it, and what I will, if You do not will it, shall never be. You created the servants for what You already knew, and within that knowledge the young and old proceed. Among them are the wretched and among them the blessed, among them the ugly and among them the beautiful. Upon this one You bestowed grace, and that one You forsook; this one You aided, and that one You did not aid.
context: This poem expresses al-Shafi'i's theological position on divine will (mashee'a) and predestination (qadr), affirming that all outcomes are determined by God's will and foreknowledge. It reflects the Ash'ari theological framework that became closely associated with the Shafi'i school of jurisprudence.

titleAr: إذا رمت أن تحيا سليما من الردى
titleEn: If You Wish to Live Safe from Ruin

verses.sadr: إذا رمت أن تحيا سليما من الردى

translationEn: If you wish to live safe from ruin, with your religion intact and your honor preserved, then let not your tongue utter any indecency, for you are full of faults and people have eyes. Associate with others in goodness, forgive those who wrong you, and defend yourself — but in the way that is best.
context: Al-Shafi'i offers practical wisdom for living with integrity: guard your tongue, be aware of your own faults, and respond to aggression with what is better. The final verse echoes the Quranic injunction (41:34) to repel evil with good.

titleEn: Indeed God Has Perceptive Servants
translationEn: Indeed God has perceptive servants who abandoned this world and feared tribulation. They looked into it, and when they realized it is no lasting homeland for the living, they made it a vast ocean and took righteous deeds as their ships upon it.
context: One of al-Shafi'i's most famous short poems, it uses the powerful metaphor of the world as a vast ocean and righteous deeds as ships to navigate it safely. The poem distills the essence of Islamic asceticism: the wise recognize the impermanence of worldly life and prepare accordingly.

titleEn: Weigh Those Who Weigh You
translationEn: Weigh those who weigh you by the same measure they use to weigh you — and what they weigh you with, weigh them by it. Whoever comes to you, go to him; whoever shuns you, turn away from him. Whoever thinks you are beneath him, then leave his desire and belittle him. And return to the Lord of all servants, for everything that comes to you is from Him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: shunned you / was cold to you

context: Al-Shafi'i establishes a principle of reciprocity in human relationships: treat others as they treat you, but ultimately return all matters to God. The poem balances worldly wisdom about self-respect with spiritual counsel about reliance on God.

titleEn: Some Eyes Stay Awake While Others Sleep

verses.sadr: فادرأ الهم ما استطعت عن النف
إن ربا كفاك بالأمس ما كا
verses.ajuz: ن سيكفيك في غد ما يكون

translationEn: Some eyes stay awake while others sleep, worrying over matters that may or may not come to pass. So repel worry from your soul as much as you can, for bearing too many worries is madness. Indeed, a Lord who took care of you yesterday in what was will take care of you tomorrow in what will be.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: He sufficed you / He took care of you

context: This beloved poem is one of al-Shafi'i's most widely quoted, offering comfort against anxiety by reminding the reader that excessive worry is futile. The final verse delivers a powerful theological argument: God's past care guarantees His future provision.

titleEn: I Killed My Ambitions and Gave My Soul Rest
translationEn: I killed my ambitions and gave my soul rest, for the soul, when obeyed in its desires, is debased. I revived contentment when it was dead, for in reviving it one's honor is preserved. When greed settles in a servant's heart, humiliation overtakes him and disgrace rises upon him.
context: Al-Shafi'i uses the metaphor of death and resurrection to contrast greed and contentment: ambitions must be killed while contentment must be revived. The poem articulates a core principle of Islamic ethics — that greed invariably leads to humiliation while contentment preserves one's dignity.

titleEn: I See You Branding Me with the Mark of Your Favor

verses.ajuz: كأنك كنت الأصل في يوم تكويني
من العيش تكفيني إلى يوم تكفيني

translationEn: I see you branding me with the mark of your favor, as though you were the origin of my creation. So spare me your oppressive reminders of generosity, for a morsel of sustenance suffices me until the day I am shrouded.
context: This clever two-verse poem uses masterful wordplay: "takweeni" means both "branding me" and "my creation," while "takfeeni" means both "suffices me" and "shrouds me." Al-Shafi'i rejects the burden of favors from those who lord their generosity over others, preferring a humble morsel to degrading obligation.

titleEn: Do Not Bear the Burden of One Who Reminds You of Favors
translationEn: Do not bear the burden of favors from anyone among people who reminds you of them. Choose for yourself its due share and be patient, for patience is a shield. The reminders of men's favors upon the hearts are harder to bear than the strike of spears.
context: Al-Shafi'i warns against the psychological burden of accepting favors from those who constantly remind you of their generosity. The striking comparison — that such reminders wound the heart more than spears — reveals the deep shame and indignity that comes with obligatory gratitude.

titleAr: إني معزيك لا أني على ثقة
titleEn: I Offer You Condolence, Not Because I Am Certain

verses.sadr: إني معزيك لا أني على ثقة

verses.ajuz: ولا المعزى وإن عاشا إلى حين

translationEn: I offer you condolence — not because I am certain of immortality, but because it is the way of religion. For the one offering condolence will not outlive his companion forever, nor will the one consoled, even if both live for a while.
context: This poem on condolence (ta'ziya) carries a profound philosophical observation: the one consoling is no more permanent than the deceased. Al-Shafi'i transforms a social custom into a meditation on universal mortality and the equality of all before death.

titleEn: All Sciences Besides the Quran Are a Distraction

verses.ajuz: إلا الحديث وعلم الفقه في الدين

verses.sadr: العلم ما كان فيه : قال ، حدثنا

translationEn: All sciences besides the Quran are a distraction, except for Hadith and the knowledge of jurisprudence in the religion. True knowledge is that which contains "he said" and "he narrated to us" — and whatever is beyond that is the whispering of devils.
context: This poem reflects al-Shafi'i's prioritization of Islamic sacred sciences — Quran, Hadith, and Fiqh — over all other fields of inquiry. As the founder of one of Islam's four major schools of jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh), his emphasis on the chain of narration ("he said, he narrated to us") underscores his methodology of deriving law from authenticated sources.

titleEn: When Your Winds Blow Favorably, Seize the Moment
translationEn: When your winds blow favorably, seize the moment, for after every gust comes stillness. And do not neglect doing good during that time, for you do not know when the stillness will come.
context: Al-Shafi'i uses the metaphor of favorable winds to urge action during times of prosperity, since every period of fortune is inevitably followed by calm or adversity. This poem has become proverbial in Arabic culture, frequently cited to motivate seizing opportunities while they last.

titleEn: No One Can Attain All Knowledge

verses.ajuz: ابن عم ابن عم أخي عم أبيه

translationEn: No one can attain all knowledge, not even if they sought it for a thousand years. There is no good in stuffing speech with filler — like "son of the cousin of the uncle of his father." Knowledge is truly a deep ocean, so take the best of everything from it. The wealth of the deceased is settled in full when opinions converge without doubt. And so it led him away from his right course.
context: Al-Shafi'i acknowledges the infinite depth of knowledge while urging seekers to be selective and take the best from every discipline. The poem also critiques verbose, padded speech and emphasizes clarity and essence over quantity.

titleEn: The Fool's Standing with the Scholar
translationEn: The fool's standing with the scholar is like the scholar's standing with the fool: each has no desire for the company of the other, and each is even more averse to the other than the other is to him. When wretchedness overcomes a fool, he becomes obstinate in opposing the scholar.
context: This poem observes the mutual aversion between the wise and the foolish, noting that each avoids the other. Al-Shafi'i adds that the worst outcome of foolishness is when it manifests as stubborn opposition to learned scholars.

titleEn: When in a Gathering We Mention Ali
translationEn: When in a gathering we mention Ali, his two grandsons, and the pure Fatima, they say: "Move past this, O people, for this is talk of the Rafidites." I disavow before the All-Protector any people who consider loving the Prophet's family to be Rafidism.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I disavow / I am free of
the family of Fatima / Ahl al-Bayt

context: This famous poem demonstrates al-Shafi'i's defense of loving the Prophet's family (Ahl al-Bayt) as a duty for all Muslims, not a sectarian position. It is often cited as evidence of the mainstream Sunni position that venerating Ali, Fatima, Hasan, and Husayn is part of orthodox Islam.

titleEn: Turn Away from the Ignorant Fool
translationEn: Turn away from the ignorant fool, for everything he says reflects upon himself. The Euphrates has never been harmed by some dogs wading through it.
context: Al-Shafi'i uses the majestic image of the Euphrates river to illustrate that the insults of fools cannot diminish a person of substance. The metaphor suggests that responding to ignorance only lowers oneself, while silence preserves one's dignity like a great river undisturbed by dogs.

titleAr: وعين الرضا عن كل عيب كليلة
titleEn: The Eye of Approval Is Blind to Every Fault

verses.sadr: وعين الرضا عن كل عيب كليلة

verses.ajuz: ولست أرى للمرء ما لا يرى ليا

verses.sadr: فإن تدن مني ، تدن منك مودتي
verses.ajuz: وأن تنأ عني ، تلقني عنك نائيا

translationEn: The eye of approval is blind to every fault, but the eye of anger reveals all flaws. I do not hold in awe one who does not hold me in awe, and I do not see for a man what he does not see for me. If you draw near to me, my affection draws near to you; if you distance yourself from me, you will find me equally distant. Each of us can do without the other during his lifetime, and when we die, we shall be even more independent of each other.
context: This poem articulates a philosophy of reciprocity and self-respect that has become proverbial in Arabic culture. The opening verse about the eyes of approval and anger is among the most quoted lines in Arabic poetry, capturing the universal truth that our perception of others is shaped by our feelings toward them.

titleAr: أرى حمرا ترعى وتعلف ما تهوى
titleEn: I See Donkeys Grazing and Fed What They Desire

verses.sadr: أرى حمرا ترعى وتعلف ما تهوى
verses.ajuz: وأسدا جياعا تظمأ الدهر لا تروى
وليس على مر القضا أحد يقوى

translationEn: I see donkeys grazing and fed whatever they desire, while lions go hungry and thirst forever without being quenched. The nobles of a people cannot obtain their sustenance, while base folk eat manna and quails. It is the pre-ordained decree of the Judge of all creation, and no one has power over the passage of fate. So whoever knows the treacherous world and its turns bears affliction with patience and does not display complaint.
context: Al-Shafi'i uses the powerful contrast between lions and donkeys to reflect on the apparent injustice of worldly provision, where the undeserving often prosper while the noble go hungry. The poem resolves this tension through acceptance of divine decree and patience in the face of affliction.
